g.data-narration: Welcome to Signal Sets in Digital Communication - the foundation of modern modulation techniques.
g.text: Understanding M-ary Modulation and Signal Space Theory

g.data-narration: A signal set is a collection of M distinct waveforms, each representing unique digital information for transmission.
g.text: A collection of M distinct waveforms {s₁(t), s₂(t), ..., sₘ(t)}
Each signal carries log₂M bits per symbol (e.g., QPSK: 2 bits/symbol)
Foundation of all digital modulation: BPSK, QPSK, M-PSK, M-QAM, M-FSK

g.data-narration: Signal sets enable higher data rates within limited bandwidth by transmitting multiple bits per symbol efficiently.
g.text: • Bandwidth Efficiency: Transmit log₂M bits using same bandwidth as binary
• Higher Data Rates: M=16 → 4× faster than binary for same symbol rate
• Noise Immunity: Larger Euclidean distance between signals = better error performance

g.data-narration: Signal space representation converts signals to vectors in N-dimensional space using orthonormal basis functions.
g.text: Any signal sᵢ(t) can be represented as vector: sᵢ = [sᵢ₁, sᵢ₂, ..., sᵢₙ]
Orthonormal basis: φ₁(t), φ₂(t), ... such that ∫φᵢφⱼdt = δᵢⱼ

g.data-narration: Gram-Schmidt procedure generates orthonormal basis functions from any set of linearly independent signals.
g.text: Step 2: s̃₂ = s₂ - (∫s₂φ₁)φ₁, then φ₂ = s̃₂/√∫|s̃₂|²
Continue: s̃ₙ = sₙ - Σ(∫sₙφᵢ)φᵢ for i=1 to n-1

g.data-narration: Orthogonal signals have zero inner product, achieved commonly in M-FSK where each frequency is distinct.
g.text: Condition: ⟨sᵢ, sⱼ⟩ = 0 for i ≠ j (zero correlation)
Example: M-FSK uses different frequencies f₁, f₂, ... fₘ

g.data-narration: Biorthogonal sets combine M/2 orthogonal signals with their negatives, reducing dimensionality while maintaining distance.
g.text: M signals: M/2 orthogonal + their negatives (antipodal pairs)
Dimensionality: N = M/2 (vs M for orthogonal)

g.data-narration: Signal constellations map signal points in the I-Q plane. Minimum Euclidean distance determines noise immunity.
g.text: Geometric arrangement of M signal points in signal space
Minimum Distance: dₘᵢₙ = min||sᵢ - sⱼ|| determines noise immunity
Larger dₘᵢₙ → Lower error probability at same SNR

g.data-narration: BPSK uses antipodal signaling in 1D space with two opposite phase signals representing binary 0 and 1.
g.text: Basis function: φ₁(t) = √(2/T)cos(2πfct), Signal vectors: s₁ = [-√Eₛ], s₂ = [+√Eₛ]

g.data-narration: QPSK uses 4 signals at 90° intervals in 2D space, transmitting 2 bits per symbol at 45°, 135°, 225°, 315°.
g.text: M=4, 2 bits/symbol. Signal points at angles 45°, 135°, 225°, 315°

g.data-narration: M-PSK places M points equally spaced on a circle. As M increases, minimum distance decreases for fixed energy.
g.text: All M signal points on circle of radius √Eₛ, equally spaced at angle 2π/M

g.data-narration: M-QAM combines amplitude and phase variations in a rectangular grid, offering higher spectral efficiency than M-PSK.
g.text: Rectangular constellation with amplitude and phase variations
Minimum distance: dₘᵢₙ = 2√(Eₛ/(M-1)) for square M-QAM

g.data-narration: Higher M increases bandwidth efficiency but requires more power for same error rate. Trade-offs are fundamental in system design.
g.text: Bandwidth Efficiency: η = log₂M bits/sec/Hz | Higher M → Better bandwidth efficiency
Power Efficiency: Higher M → Lower power efficiency (needs more SNR for same error rate)
Error Performance: dₘᵢₙ decreases as M increases → Higher Pₛ at same Eₛ/N₀

g.data-narration: Remember these essential formulas for exams: minimum distances for PSK and QAM, and bandwidth efficiency calculations.
